description:
Observation:   Initially heard quietly tapping deep in a mature juniper tree (? sp.).  When located could only make out a dark silhouette, but recognized it was clearly a sapsucker species (Sphyrapicus).  After a couple of minutes of obscured viewing
at ~5m (15ft) with binoculars (8X40 Nikon), the bird moved to an exposed perch
in an adjacent locust (? sp.) for an excellent clear view at ~6m (20ft) in
bright overcast light.  Total observation
lasted about 10 minutes.
 Description:    Medium sized, well proportion woodpecker
with a medium weight and length, sharp, and slightly upturned bill.  Basically sapsucker size and shape.
Plumage:  Primarily solid glossy black overall with
lemon yellow belly and large bright white wing patches.
Head: Head and upper chest black with the exception of bright white malar and post auricular stripes on the face, and a small, deep red chin and throat patch (entirely surrounded by black).
Upperparts: Back entirely solid
black. Large bright white patches on the upper wing, visible when
perched.  White rump.
Underparts: Upper chest black and sharply demarcated from lemon yellow lower chest.  Lower belly paler lemon yellow with scalloped greyish yellow flanks.
Tail: Solid black.

Bill / Feet / Eye Color:  Bill about ½ to ¼ head length, medium weight, sharp, and slightly upturned.  Eyes, bill, and feet all dark 
                           Structure:  Well proportioned, medium sized woodpecker
with round head and medium length tail.
